catechisms.question: What is the chief end of man?
catechisms.answer: . 1 The chief end of man is to glorify God and to enjoy Him forever.

scriptures.text: For from Him [as their origin] and through Him [as their effectual means]  and to Him [as their ultimate end] are all things. To Him be the glory forever. Amen
. Whether therefore ye eat, or drink, or whatsoever ye do, do all to the glory of God
And besides You, I desire nothing on earth. My flesh and my heart fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever

catechisms.raw_text:
What is the chief end of man?
For from Him [as their origin] and through Him [as 
their effectual means]  and to Him [as their ultimate 
end] are all things. To Him be the glory forever. 
Amen (NASB; Rom. 11:36).
Whether therefore ye eat, or drink, or whatsoever ye 
do, do all to the glory of God (KJV; I Cor. 10:31).
Whom have I in heaven but You? And besides You, I 
desire nothing on earth. My flesh and my heart fail, but 
God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever 
(NASB; Ps. 73:25-26).
1 
The chief end of man is to glorify God 
and to enjoy Him forever.
(Westminster Shorter Catechism , Q. 1)

catechisms.question: God and to enjoy Him forever?
catechisms.answer: . 2 In order to glorify God and to enjoy Him forever, I must know and worship Him in spirit and in truth.

scriptures.text: . . . know the God of your father, and serve [or, worship] Him with a whole heart and a willing mind . . .
. In all your ways acknowledge [know] Him . . .
. . . . the true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit [my innermost being] and truth [His revealed will]; for such people the Father seeks to worship Him

catechisms.raw_text:
What must I do in order to glorify 
God and to enjoy Him forever?
. . . know the God of your father, and serve [or, worship] Him 
with a whole heart and a willing mind . . .  (NASB; I Chron. 28:9).
In all your ways acknowledge [know] Him . . . (NASB; Prov. 3:6). 
. . . the true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit 
[my innermost being] and truth [His revealed will]; for 
such people the Father seeks to worship Him 
(NASB; John 4:23).
2 
In order to glorify God and to enjoy 
Him forever, I must know and worship 
Him in spirit and in truth.

catechisms.question: How can I know about God?
catechisms.answer: . 3 I can know about God by His creation, preservation, and government of the universe; which are before my eyes as a most elegant book, wherein all creatures, great and small, are as so many characters leading us to contemplate “the invisible things of God. ” (Belgic Confession, Article 2, quoting Rom. 1:20)

scriptures.text: By His creation:  The heavens are telling the glory of God; and their expanse is declaring the works of His hands. Day to day pours forth speech, and night to night reveals knowledge
. By His preservation:  The eyes of all look to You, and You give them their food in due time. You open Your hand and satisfy the desire of every living thing
Let him give heed to these things [see Psalm 107:1-42], and consider the lovinkindnesses of the LORD

catechisms.raw_text:
How can I know about God?
By His creation:  The heavens are telling the glory of God; 
and their expanse is declaring the works of His hands. Day 
to day pours forth speech, and night to night reveals 
knowledge (NASB; Ps. 19:1-2).
By His preservation:  The eyes of all look to You, and You 
give them their food in due time. You open Your hand and 
satisfy the desire of every living thing (NASB; Ps. 145:15-16).
By His government:  Who is wise? Let him give heed 
to these things [see Psalm 107:1-42], and consider the 
lovinkindnesses of the LORD (NASB; Ps. 107:43).
3 
I can know about God by His creation, 
preservation, and government of the 
universe; which are before my eyes as a
most elegant book, wherein all creatures,
great and small, are as so many characters
leading us to contemplate “the invisible 
things of God. ”  
(Belgic Confession, Article 2, quoting Rom. 1:20)

catechisms.question: completely?
catechisms.answer: . 4 I can know about God most completely by the holy scriptures.

scriptures.text: But He said, “You cannot see My face, for no man can see Me and live!” . . .  Then the LORD passed by in front of him and proclaimed. . . ”
. How blessed are those who observe His testimonies, who seek Him with all their heart
. [Observing His testimonies is to seek and find Him.] Search the scriptures . . . they are they which testify of me

catechisms.raw_text:
How can I know about God most 
completely?
But He said, “You cannot see My face, for no man can see 
Me and live!” . . .  Then the LORD passed by in front of him 
and proclaimed. . . ” (NASB; Ex. 33:20; 34:6-7).
How blessed are those who observe His testimonies, 
who seek Him with all their heart (NASB; Ps. 119:2). 
[Observing His testimonies is to seek and find Him.]
Search the scriptures . . . they are they which testify 
of me (KJV; John 5:39).
4 
I can know about God most completely 
by the holy scriptures.

catechisms.question: What are the holy scriptures?
catechisms.answer: . 5 The holy scriptures are the inerrant Word of God, the Old and New Testaments, the only rule of my faith and obedience, the only rule to direct me how I may glorify God and enjoy Him forever.

scriptures.text: . . . the gospel of God, which He promised beforehand through His prophets in the holy Scriptures
. . . . the Scriptures of the prophets . . .
. . . . Paul, according to the wisdom given him, wrote to you, as also in all his letters, . . . which the untaught and unstable distort, as they do also the rest of the Scriptures

. All Scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in righteousness: That the man of God may be perfect, thoroughly furnished unto all good works

catechisms.raw_text:
What are the holy scriptures?
. . . the gospel of God, which He promised beforehand through 
His prophets in the holy Scriptures (NASB; Rom. 1:1b-2).
. . . the Scriptures of the prophets . . . (NASB; Matt. 26:56).
. . . Paul, according to the wisdom given him, wrote to you, 
as also in all his letters, . . . which the untaught and unstable 
distort, as they do also the rest of the Scriptures 
(NASB; II Pet. 3:15-16). 
All Scripture is given by inspiration of God, and is profitable
for doctrine, for reproof, for correction, for instruction in 
righteousness: That the man of God may be perfect, thoroughly 
furnished unto all good works (KJV; II Tim. 3:16-17).
5 
The holy scriptures are the inerrant 
Word of God, the Old and New 
Testaments, the only rule of my faith
and obedience, the only rule to direct 
me how I may glorify God and enjoy 
Him forever.
(Adapted from  Westminster Shorter Catechism, Q. 2)

catechisms.question: scriptures are inerrant?
catechisms.answer: . 6 We know that the holy scriptures are inerrant because they claim to be, and because the Spirit of God bears witness with them in our hearts.

scriptures.text: For truly I say to you, until heaven and earth pass away, not the smallest letter or stroke shall pass from the Law until all is accomplished
. . . . the Scripture cannot be broken [by being proved false]
. Now we have received, not the spirit of the world, but the Spirit who is from God, so that we may know the things freely given to us by God

catechisms.raw_text:
How do we know that the holy 
scriptures are inerrant?
For truly I say to you, until heaven and earth pass away, 
not the smallest letter or stroke shall pass from the Law 
until all is accomplished (NASB; Matt. 5:18).
. . . the Scripture cannot be broken [by being proved false] 
(NASB; John 10:35).
Now we have received, not the spirit of the world, but the 
Spirit who is from God, so that we may know the things 
freely given to us by God (NASB; I Cor. 2:12).
6 
We know that the holy scriptures
are inerrant because they claim to be,
and because the Spirit of God bears 
witness with them in our hearts.

catechisms.question: scriptures?
catechisms.answer: . 7 God gave us the holy scriptures as “men moved by the Holy Spirit spoke from God.”

scriptures.text: All scripture is given by inspiration of God . . .

. But know this first of all, that no prophecy of Scripture is [came into being] a matter of one’s own interpretation, for no prophecy was ever made by an act of human will, but men moved by the Holy Spirit spoke from God

catechisms.raw_text:
How did God give us the holy 
scriptures?
All scripture is given by inspiration of God . . . 
(KJV; II Tim. 3:16).
But know this first of all, that no prophecy of Scripture is 
[came into being] a matter of one’s own interpretation, 
for no prophecy was ever made by an act of human 
will, but men moved by the Holy Spirit spoke from God 
(NASB; II Pet. 1:20-21).
7 
God gave us the holy scriptures as 
“men moved by the Holy Spirit
spoke from God.”

catechisms.question: scriptures?
catechisms.answer: . 8 We are to interpret the holy scriptures by the infallible scriptures themselves; and therefore, when there is a question about the true and full sense of any Scripture (which is not manifold, but one), it must be searched and known by other places that speak more clearly. (Westminster Confession of Faith, I. 9)

scriptures.text: Simeon has related . . . With this the words of the Prophets agree, just as it is written. . .
. Which things we also speak, not in words taught by human wisdom, but in those taught by the Spirit, combining spiritual thoughts with spiritual words

catechisms.raw_text:
How are we to interpret the holy 
scriptures?
Simeon has related . . . With this the words of the 
Prophets agree, just as it is written. . . (NASB; Acts 15:15).
Which things we also speak, not in words taught by 
human wisdom, but in those taught by the Spirit, 
combining spiritual thoughts with spiritual words 
(NASB; I Cor. 2:13).
8 
We are to interpret the holy scriptures 
by the infallible scriptures themselves; 
and therefore, when there is a question 
about the true and full sense of any 
Scripture (which is not manifold, but one), 
it must be searched and known by other 
places that speak more clearly.  
(Westminster Confession of Faith, I. 9)

catechisms.question: worship God in spirit and in truth?
catechisms.answer: . 9 According to the holy scriptures, the only way for me to know and worship God in spirit and in truth is through Jesus Christ, His only begotten Son.

scriptures.text: No man hath seen God at any time: the only begotten Son, which is in the bosom of the Father, he hath declared [exegeted] him
. And we know that the Son of God has come, and has given us understanding so that we may know Him who is true. . .
. Jesus saith unto him, I am the way, the truth, and the life: no man cometh unto the Father, but by me

catechisms.raw_text:
According to the holy scriptures, what 
is the only way for me to know and 
worship God in spirit and in truth?
No man hath seen God at any time: the only begotten Son, 
which is in the bosom of the Father, he hath declared 
[exegeted] him (KJV; John 1:18).
And we know that the Son of God has come, and has given 
us understanding so that we may know Him who is true. . . 
(NASB; I John 5:20).
Jesus saith unto him, I am the way, the truth, and the life: 
no man cometh unto the Father, but by me (KJV; John 14:6).
9 
According to the holy scriptures, 
the only way for me to know and 
worship God in spirit and in truth
is through Jesus Christ, His only 
begotten Son.

catechisms.question: who is God?
catechisms.answer: . 10 According to the holy scriptures, God is, “I AM WHO I AM,” “the LORD,” “the LORD God.”

scriptures.text: Then Moses said to God . . .  “Now they may say to me, ‘What is His name?’   What shall I say to them?” God said to Moses,  “I AM WHO I AM. . . ”

. God, furthermore, said to Moses, “Thus you shall say to the sons of Israel, ‘The LORD, the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has sent me to you. ’   This is My name forever, and this is My memorial-name to all generations
. The LORD God made the earth and the heavens

catechisms.raw_text:
According to the holy scriptures, 
who is God?
Then Moses said to God . . .  “Now they may say to me, 
‘What is His name?’   What shall I say to them?” God said to 
Moses,  “I AM WHO I AM. . . ”  (NASB; Exod. 3:13-14).
God, furthermore, said to Moses, “Thus you shall say to the 
sons of Israel, ‘The LORD, the God of your fathers, the God 
of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has 
sent me to you. ’   This is My name forever, and this is My 
memorial-name to all generations (NASB; Exod. 3:15. Also 
see Isaiah 42:8.). 
The LORD God made the earth and the heavens (KJV; Gen. 2:4).
10 
According to the holy scriptures, 
God is, “I AM WHO I AM,” 
“the LORD,” “the LORD God.”

catechisms.question: what is God?
catechisms.answer: . He is infinite (II Chron. 6:18; Job 11:7-9), eternal (Ps. 90:2), and unchangeable (Mal. 3:6; Jam. 1:17) in His being and in all His perfections. 11 According to the holy scriptures, God is a Spirit, infinite, eternal, and unchangeable, in His being, wisdom, power, holiness, justice, goodness, and truth.

scriptures.text: God is spirit [or, Spirit] . . .

catechisms.raw_text:
According to the holy scriptures, 
what is God?
God is spirit [or, Spirit] . . . (NASB; John 4:24).
He is infinite (II Chron. 6:18; Job 11:7-9), eternal (Ps. 90:2), 
and unchangeable (Mal. 3:6; Jam. 1:17) in His being and 
in all His perfections.   
11 
According to the holy scriptures, 
God is a Spirit, infinite, eternal, 
and unchangeable, in His being, 
wisdom, power, holiness, justice, 
goodness, and truth.  
(Westminster Shorter Catechism , Q. 4)

catechisms.question: How many Gods are there?
catechisms.answer: . (Belgic Confession (1561) Article 1;  Second Helvetic Confession III. 2. (1566))

scriptures.text: The LORD our God is one LORD

. “You are My witnesses, ” declares the LORD, “And My servant whom I have chosen, so that you may know and believe Me and understand that I am He. Before Me there was no God formed, and there will be none after Me. I, even I, am the LORD, and there is no savior besides Me”

. “Turn to Me and be saved, all the ends of the earth; For I am God, and there is no other.  I have sworn by Myself, the word has gone forth from My mouth in righ- teousness and will not turn back, that to Me every knee will bow, every tongue will swear allegiance”

. 12 We all believe with the heart, and confess with the mouth, that there is only one living and true God.  And we detest the multitude of gods, because it is expressly written, “The LORD our God is one LORD”
, and, “There is no other God besides Me”

catechisms.raw_text:
How many Gods are there?
The LORD our God is one LORD (KJV; Deut. 6:4).
“You are My witnesses, ” declares the LORD, “And My 
servant whom I have chosen, so that you may know and 
believe Me and understand that I am He. Before Me there 
was no God formed, and there will be none after Me. I, 
even I, am the LORD, and there is no savior besides Me” 
(NASB; Is. 43:10-11).
“Turn to Me and be saved, all the ends of the earth;  
For I am God, and there is no other.  I have sworn by 
Myself, the word has gone forth from My mouth in righ-
teousness and will not turn back, that to Me every knee 
will bow, every tongue will swear allegiance” 
(NASB; Is. 45:22-23).   
12 
We all believe with the heart, and confess 
with the mouth, that there is only one 
living and true God.  And we detest the 
multitude of gods, because it is expressly 
written, “The LORD our God is one LORD” 
(KJV; Deut. 6:4), and, “There is no other God 
besides Me” (NASB; Is. 45:21). 
(Belgic Confession (1561) Article 1;  Second Helvetic 
Confession III. 2. (1566))

catechisms.question: in three persons?
catechisms.answer: . 13 We know that God exists in three persons because they are distinguished in Scripture by their personal relations with one another and by their distinctive works. Question:  What are two of the distinctive works of each of the persons of the Godhead?

scriptures.text: . . . baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit

. . . . God sent forth His Son, born of a woman, born under the Law, so that He might redeem those who were under the Law, that we might receive the adoption as sons. Because you are sons, God has sent forth the Spirit of His Son into our hearts, crying, “Abba!  Father!”

catechisms.raw_text:
How do we know that God exists 
in three persons?
. . . baptizing them in the name of the Father and the 
Son and the Holy Spirit (NASB; Matt. 28:19).
. . . God sent forth His Son, born of a woman, born under 
the Law, so that He might redeem those who were under 
the Law, that we might receive the adoption as sons.  
Because you are sons, God has sent forth the Spirit of 
His Son into our hearts, crying, “Abba!  Father!” 
(NASB; Gal. 4:4-6; compare Rom. 8:15).
  
13 
We know that God exists in three 
persons because they are distinguished 
in Scripture by their personal relations 
with one another and by their 
distinctive works.  
Question:  What are two of the distinctive 
works of each of the persons of the Godhead?

catechisms.question: Who is God the Father?
catechisms.answer: . 14 God the Father is the eternal Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, Who so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that by faith we might become His little children (1 John 2:1, 12; 3:18) and He our heavenly Father.

scriptures.text: Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies and God of all comfort
. We have seen and testify that the Father has sent the Son to be the Savior of the World
. For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have everlasting life
. But as many as received Him, to them He gave the right to become children of God, even to those who believe in His name

catechisms.raw_text:
Who is God the Father?
Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, 
the Father of mercies and God of all comfort 
(NASB; II Cor. 1:3).  
We have seen and testify that the Father has sent the 
Son to be the Savior of the World (NASB; 1 John 4:14).
For God so loved the world, that he gave his only 
begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should 
not perish, but have everlasting life (KJV; John 3:16).
But as many as received Him, to them He gave the right 
to become children of God, even to those who believe 
in His name (NASB; John 1:12).
14 
God the Father is the eternal Father 
of our Lord Jesus Christ, Who so 
loved the world that He gave His 
only begotten Son, that by faith we 
might become His little children 
(1 John 2:1, 12; 3:18) and He our 
heavenly Father.

catechisms.question: Who is God the Son?
catechisms.answer: . 15 God the Son is “the only begotten from the Father,” “the radiance of His glory and the exact representation of His nature,” Who, being the eternal Son of God, became man, and so was, and continues to be, God and man in two distinct natures, and one person, forever.

scriptures.text: . . . and we saw His glory, glory as of the only begotten from the Father, full of grace and truth
. And He is the radiance of His glory and the exact representation of His nature . . .
. And the Word became flesh, and dwelt among us . . .
. . . . Christ according to the flesh, who is over all, God bless- ed forever. Amen
. For in Him all the fullness of Deity dwells in bodily form

catechisms.raw_text:
Who is God the Son?
. . . and we saw His glory, glory as of the only begotten 
from the Father, full of grace and truth (NASB; John 1:14).
And He is the radiance of His glory and the exact 
representation of His nature . . . (NASB; Heb. 1:3).
And the Word became flesh, and dwelt among us . . .  
(NASB; John 1:14).
. . . Christ according to the flesh, who is over all, God bless-
ed forever. Amen (NASB; Rom. 9:5).
For in Him all the fullness of Deity dwells in bodily form 
(NASB; Col. 2:9).
15 
God the Son is “the only begotten from the 
Father,” “the radiance of His glory and the 
exact representation of His nature,” Who, 
being the eternal Son of God, became man, 
and so was, and continues to be, God and 
man in two distinct natures, and one 
person, forever.
(Westminster Shorter Catechism ,  Q. 21)

catechisms.question: Who is God the Holy Spirit?
catechisms.answer: . John 14:16; Romans 8:15-17 16 God the Holy Spirit is the eternal Spirit proceeding from the Father, sent by the Father and the Son, but of one and the same essence, majesty and glory with both; and given me, to make  me by a true faith, partaker of Christ and all His benefits, that He may comfort me and abide with me forever. (Belgic Confession (1561), Article 11; Heidelberg Catechism (1563), Q.  53)

scriptures.text: How much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered Himself without blemish to God, cleanse your conscience from dead works to serve the living God
When the Helper comes, whom I will send to you from the Father [see John 14:26], that is the Spirit of truth who proceeds from the Father, He will testify about Me
. . . . baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit

catechisms.raw_text:
Who is God the Holy Spirit?
How much more will the blood of Christ, who through 
the eternal Spirit offered Himself without blemish to 
God, cleanse your conscience from dead works to serve 
the living God (NASB; Heb. 9:14)?
When the Helper comes, whom I will send to you from 
the Father [see John 14:26], that is the Spirit of truth 
who proceeds from the Father, He will testify about Me 
(NASB; John 15:26).
. . . baptizing them in the name of the Father and the 
Son and the Holy Spirit (NASB; Matt. 28:19).
John 14:16; Romans 8:15-17
16 
God the Holy Spirit is the eternal Spirit 
proceeding from the Father, sent by the 
Father and the Son, but of one and the 
same essence, majesty and glory with both; 
and given me, to make  me by a true faith, 
partaker of Christ and all His benefits, that He 
may comfort me and abide with me forever.  
(Belgic Confession (1561), Article 11; Heidelberg 
Catechism (1563), Q.  53)

catechisms.question: persons of the Godhead one?
catechisms.answer: . 17 The three persons of the Godhead are one in that they are a single, eternal, indivisible Being, the same in substance, equal in power and glory. , Q.  6)

scriptures.text: . . . baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit
. I and the Father are one
Acts 5:3a, 4b). The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit, be with you all

catechisms.raw_text:
In what sense are the three 
persons of the Godhead one?
. . . baptizing them in the name of the Father and the 
Son and the Holy Spirit (NASB; Matt. 28:19).
I and the Father are one (NASB; John 10:30).
But Peter said, “Ananias, why has Satan filled your heart 
to lie to the Holy Spirit? . . . You have not lied to men 
but to God” (Acts 5:3a, 4b). 
The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, 
and the fellowship of the Holy Spirit, be with you all 
(NASB; II Cor. 13:14).
17 
The three persons of the Godhead are 
one in that they are a single, eternal, 
indivisible Being, the same in substance, 
equal in power and glory.
 
(Westminster Shorter Catechism (1647), Q.  6)

catechisms.question: things?
catechisms.answer: . 18 God is related to all other things as their Creator, Sustainer, and Sovereign. He does by them, for them, or upon them whatever He pleases, and in doing so He is most holy in all his counsels, in all His works, and in all His commands. (Westminster Confession of Faith (1647), II. 2)

scriptures.text: Worthy are you, our Lord and our God, to receive glory and honor and power; for You created all things, and because of Your will they existed and were created
. He [God’s Son] is before all things, and in Him all things hold together
. . . . He who is the blessed and only Sovereign, the King of kings and Lord of lords

catechisms.raw_text:
How is God related to all other 
things?
Worthy are you, our Lord and our God, to receive glory 
and honor and power; for You created all things, and 
because of Your will they existed and were created 
(NASB; Rev. 4:11). 
He [God’s Son] is before all things, and in Him all things 
hold together (NASB; Col. 1:17; cf. Heb. 1:3).
. . . He who is the blessed and only Sovereign, the King 
of kings and Lord of lords (NASB; I Tim. 6:15).
He does according to His will in the host of heaven 
and among the inhabitants of earth; and no one can 
ward off His hand or say to Him, “What have you done?” 
(NASB; Dan. 4:35). 
18 
God is related to all other things as 
their Creator, Sustainer, and Sovereign. 
He does by them, for them, or upon them 
whatever He pleases, and in doing so He 
is most holy in all his counsels, in all His 
works, and in all His commands. 
(Westminster Confession of Faith (1647), II. 2)

catechisms.question: What is God’s work of creation?
catechisms.answer: . 19 God’s work of creation is His making all things of nothing, by the word of His power, in the space of six [literal] days, and all very good.

scriptures.text: In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth
. . . . God, who gives life to the dead and calls into being that which does not exist
. By faith we understand that the worlds [ages] were prepared by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things which are visible
. For in six days the LORD made the heavens and the earth, the sea and all that is in them. . .
. God saw all that He had made, and behold, it was very good.  And there was evening and there was morning, the sixth day.  Thus the heavens and the earth were completed, and all their hosts

catechisms.raw_text:
What is God’s work of creation?
In the beginning, God created the heavens and the 
earth (NASB; Gen. 1:2).
. . . God, who gives life to the dead and calls into being 
that which does not exist (NASB; Rom. 4:17).
By faith we understand that the worlds [ages] were 
prepared by the word of God, so that what is seen was 
not made out of things which are visible (NASB; Heb. 11:3). 
For in six days the LORD made the heavens and the 
earth, the sea and all that is in them. . . (NASB; Ex. 20:11).
God saw all that He had made, and behold, it was very 
good.  And there was evening and there was morning, 
the sixth day.  Thus the heavens and the earth were 
completed, and all their hosts (NASB; Gen. 1:31-2:1). 
19 
God’s work of creation is His making 
all things of nothing, by the word of 
His power, in the space of six [literal]
days, and all very good.  
(Westminster Shorter Catechism, Q. 9, with editing)

catechisms.question: How did God create man?
catechisms.answer: . 20 God created man male and female, after His own image, in knowledge, righteousness, and holiness, with dominion over the creatures.

scriptures.text: Then God said, “Let Us make man in Our image, according to Our likeness; and let them rule over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the sky and over the cattle and over all the earth, and over every creeping thing that creeps on the earth. God created man in His own image, in the image of God He created Him; male and female He created them. God blessed them; and God said to them, “Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the earth, and subdue it; and rule over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the sky and over every living thing that moves on the earth”
. . . . and have put on the new self who is being renewed to a true knowledge according to the image of the One who created him
. . . . and put on the new self, which in the likeness of God has been created in righteousness and holiness of the truth

catechisms.raw_text:
How did God create man?
Then God said, “Let Us make man in Our image, according to 
Our likeness; and let them rule over the fish of the sea and over 
the birds of the sky and over the cattle and over all the earth, 
and over every creeping thing that creeps on the earth.
God created man in His own image, in the image of God He 
created Him; male and female He created them. God blessed 
them; and God said to them, “Be fruitful and multiply, and fill 
the earth, and subdue it; and rule over the fish of the sea and 
over the birds of the sky and over every living thing that moves 
on the earth” (NASB; Gen. 1:26-28).
. . . and have put on the new self who is being renewed to a 
true knowledge according to the image of the One who created 
him (NASB; Col. 3:10).
. . . and put on the new self, which in the likeness of God has been 
created in righteousness and holiness of the truth (NASB; Eph. 4:24). 
20 
God created man male and female, 
after His own image, in knowledge, 
righteousness, and holiness, with 
dominion over the creatures.   
(Westminster Shorter Catechism, Q. 10)

catechisms.question: What is marriage of God’s making?
catechisms.answer: . 21 Marriage of God’s making is the joining by covenant of one male and one female to become one flesh until parted by death. Note: Male and female are the two genders created by God and multiplied naturally by their sexual union.  Male and female  He created them. God blessed them; and God said to them, “Be fruitful and multiply” (Gen. 1:27b-28a).

scriptures.text: The LORD God fashioned into a woman the rib which He had taken from the man, and brought her to the man.  The man said, “This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my flesh; she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out of Man. ”  For this reason a man shall leave his father and his mother, and be joined to his wife; and they shall become one flesh
. . . . she is your companion and your wife by covenant
. So they are no longer two, but one flesh.  What therefore God has joined together, let no man separate
. For the married woman is bound by law to her husband while he is living; but if her husband dies, she is released from the law concerning the husband

catechisms.raw_text:
What is marriage of God’s making?
The LORD God fashioned into a woman the rib which He 
had taken from the man, and brought her to the man.  The 
man said, “This is now bone of my bones, and flesh of my 
flesh; she shall be called Woman, because she was taken out 
of Man. ”  For this reason a man shall leave his father and his 
mother, and be joined to his wife; and they shall become 
one flesh (NASB; Gen. 2:22-24).
. . . she is your companion and your wife by covenant 
(NASB; Mal. 2:14). 
So they are no longer two, but one flesh.  What therefore God 
has joined together, let no man separate (NASB; Matt. 19:6).
For the married woman is bound by law to her husband 
while he is living; but if her husband dies, she is released 
from the law concerning the husband (NASB; Rom. 7:2). 
 
21 
Marriage of God’s making is the joining by 
covenant of one male and one female to 
become one flesh until parted by death. 
Note: Male and female are the two genders 
created by God and multiplied naturally by their 
sexual union.  Male and female  He created them.  
God blessed them; and God said to them, “Be fruitful 
and multiply” (Gen. 1:27b-28a).

catechisms.question: image?
catechisms.answer: . Behold, I was brought forth in iniquity, and in sin my mother conceived me (Ps. 51:5). 22 The cause of the death and depravity of man made in God’s image, is the fall and disobedience of our first parents, Adam and Eve, so that our nature became so corrupt that we are all conceived and born in sin.

scriptures.text: When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was desirable to make one wise, she took from its fruit and ate; and she gave also to her husband with her, and he ate
. . . . through one man sin entered into the world, and death through sin, and so death spread to all men, because all sinned

catechisms.raw_text:
What is the cause of the death and 
depravity of man, made in God’s 
image?
When the woman saw that the tree was good for food, and 
that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was 
desirable to make one wise, she took from its fruit and ate; 
and she gave also to her husband with her, and he ate 
(NASB; Gen. 3:6).
. . . through one man sin entered into the world, and death 
through sin, and so death spread to all men, because all 
sinned (NASB; Rom. 5:12).
Behold, I was brought forth in iniquity, and in sin my mother 
conceived me (Ps. 51:5). 
 
22 
The cause of the death and depravity of 
man made in God’s image, is the fall and 
disobedience of our first parents, Adam 
and Eve, so that our nature became so 
corrupt that we are all conceived and 
born in sin. 
(Adapted from the Heidelberg Catechism, Q. 7)

catechisms.question: we are all conceived and born?
catechisms.answer: . 23 The state of sin into which we are all conceived and born consists in the guilt of Adam’s first sin, the lack of original righteousness, the corruption of our whole nature (commonly called original sin), and all the actual transgressions which proceed from it.

we are all conceived and born?

catechisms.question: creation?
catechisms.answer: . 25 The Fall of man made in God’s image affected the rest of earthly creation by subjecting it to God’s curse of futility and slavery to corruption. Futility refers to creation’s inability to fulfill entirely that for which it was created.  Animal and vegetable life needed man for their perfecting, even before the Fall (Gen. 1:26; 2:15).  But after it, We may think of the whole magnificent theatre of the universe together with all its splendid properties and all the chorus of sub-human life, created to glorify God but unable to do so fully, so long as man the chief actor in the drama of God’s praise fails to contribute his rational part (C. E. B. Cranfield, The Epistle to the Romans).

scriptures.text: Then to Adam He said . . . “Cursed is the ground because of you . . . .  Both thorns and thistles it shall grow for you”

. For the creation was subjected to futility, not willingly, but because of Him who subjected it, in hope that the creation itself also will be set free from its slavery to corruption into the freedom of the glory of the children of God.  For we know that the whole creation groans and suffers the pains of childbirth together until now

catechisms.raw_text:
How did the Fall of man made in 
God’s image affect the rest of earthly 
creation?
Then to Adam He said . . . “Cursed is the ground because 
of you . . . .  Both thorns and thistles it shall grow for you” 
(NASB; Gen. 3:17, 18).
For the creation was subjected to futility, not willingly, but 
because of Him who subjected it, in hope that the creation 
itself also will be set free from its slavery to corruption into 
the freedom of the glory of the children of God.  For we 
know that the whole creation groans and suffers the pains 
of childbirth together until now (NASB; Rom. 8:20-22). 
 
25 
The Fall of man made in God’s image 
affected the rest of earthly creation by 
subjecting it to God’s curse of futility and 
slavery to corruption.
Futility refers to creation’s inability to fulfill entirely 
that for which it was created.  Animal and vegetable life 
needed man for their perfecting, even before the Fall 
(Gen. 1:26; 2:15).  But after it, We may think of the whole 
magnificent theatre of the universe together with all 
its splendid properties and all the chorus of sub-human 
life, created to glorify God but unable to do so fully, so 
long as man the chief actor in the drama of God’s praise 
fails to contribute his rational part (C. E. B. Cranfield, The 
Epistle to the Romans).

catechisms.question: related to God after the Fall?
catechisms.answer: . 26 After the Fall, all mankind continues to be subject to God’s providence and to owe to Him whatever He is pleased to require.

scriptures.text: All the inhabitants of the earth are accounted as nothing. But He does according to His will in the host of heaven and among the inhabitants of earth; and no one can ward off His hand or say to Him, “What have you done?”
. See Romans 2:15 combined with I Timothy 1:8-11. Instead, you ought to say, “If the Lord wills, we will live and also do this or that”

catechisms.raw_text:
How does all mankind continue to be 
related to God after the Fall?
All the inhabitants of the earth are accounted as nothing. 
But He does according to His will in the host of heaven and 
among the inhabitants of earth; and no one can ward off His 
hand or say to Him, “What have you done?” (NASB; Dan. 4:35). 
See Romans 2:15 combined with I Timothy 1:8-11.
Instead, you ought to say, “If the Lord wills, we will live and 
also do this or that” (NASB; Jam. 4:15). 
 
 
26 
After the Fall, all mankind 
continues to be subject to 
God’s providence and to owe 
to Him whatever He is pleased to 
require.

catechisms.question: What is God’s providence?
catechisms.answer: . 27 God’s providence is His most holy, wise, and powerful preserving and governing of all His creatures and all their actions, to His own glory.  Providence.  Think of the word, provide . Providence  concerns all that God provides  for His creatures; their circumstances, sustenance, preservation, government, abilities, successes, failures, usefulness, etc.

scriptures.text: The LORD has established His throne in the heavens, and His sovereignty rules over all
. Whatever the LORD pleases, He does, in heaven and in earth, in the seas and in all deeps
And yet not one of them will fall to the ground apart from your Father.  But the very hairs of your head are all numbered
. For from Him and through Him and to Him are all things.  To Him be the glory forever.  Amen

catechisms.attribution: (Adapted from the Westminster Shorter and Larger catechisms, Qs. 11, 18)
catechisms.raw_text:
What is God’s providence?
The LORD has established His throne in the heavens, and His 
sovereignty rules over all (NASB; Ps. 103:19).
Whatever the LORD pleases, He does, in heaven and in earth, 
in the seas and in all deeps (NASB; Ps. 135:6).
Are not two sparrows sold for a cent?  And yet not one of 
them will fall to the ground apart from your Father.  But 
the very hairs of your head are all numbered (NASB; Matt. 
10:29-30).
For from Him and through Him and to Him are all things.  To 
Him be the glory forever.  Amen (NASB; Rom. 11:36).
 
 
27 
God’s providence is His most holy, wise, 
and powerful preserving and governing 
of all His creatures and all their actions, 
to His own glory.
(Adapted from the Westminster Shorter and Larger 
catechisms, Qs. 11, 18)
Providence.  Think of the word, provide .  
Providence  concerns all that God provides  for 
His creatures; their circumstances, sustenance, 
preservation, government, abilities, successes, 
failures, usefulness, etc.

catechisms.question: about God’s providence?
catechisms.answer: . 28 The benefit of our knowing about God’s providence is that it makes us patient in adversity and thankful in prosperity, so that we may place our firm trust in our Heavenly Father concerning everything that befalls us.  For deeply reassuring teaching on this subject, read prayerfully John Flavel’s,The Mystery of Divine Providence.

scriptures.text: I will cry to God Most High, to God who accomplishes all things for me
And yet not one of them will fall to the ground apart from your Father.  But the very hairs of your head are all numbered.  So do not fear; you are more valuable than many sparrows
. In everything give thanks; for this is God’s will for you in Christ Jesus

catechisms.raw_text:
What is the benefit of our knowing 
about God’s providence?
I will cry to God Most High, to God who accomplishes all 
things for me (NASB; Ps. 57:2).
Are not two sparrows sold for a cent?  And yet not one of 
them will fall to the ground apart from your Father.  But the 
very hairs of your head are all numbered.  So do not fear; 
you are more valuable than many sparrows (NASB; Matt. 
10:29-31).
In everything give thanks; for this is God’s will for you in 
Christ Jesus (NASB; I Thess. 5:18). 
 
 
28 
The benefit of our knowing about God’s 
providence is that it makes us patient 
in adversity and thankful in prosperity, 
so that we may place our firm trust in our 
Heavenly Father concerning everything 
that befalls us.
(Adapted from the Heidelberg Catechism, Q. 28)
For deeply reassuring teaching on this subject, 
read prayerfully John Flavel’s,The Mystery of 
Divine Providence.

What hope has God given that it is 
possible for us to be redeemed from 
our state of sin and misery?
But the lovingkindness of the LORD is from everlasting to 
everlasting on those who fear Him, and His righteousness 
to children’s children,  to those who keep His covenant and

 . . . Israelites, to whom belongs the adoption as sons, and

covenants.

catechisms.question: misery?
catechisms.answer: . 30 God’s covenants that give us hope in our state of sin and misery are the Noahic, the Abrahamic, the Mosaic, the Davidic, and the New.

scriptures.text: I prayed to the LORD my God and confessed and said, “Alas, O Lord, the great and awesome God, who keeps His covenant and lovingkindness for those who love Him and keep His commandments, we have sinned, committed iniquity, acted wickedly and rebelled, even turning aside from Your commandments and ordinances”

What are God’s covenants that 
give us hope in our state of sin and

catechisms.question: gracious provisions?
catechisms.answer: . 32 The Abrahamic covenant’s gracious provisions are God’s promises concerning (1) Abraham’s physical descendants, especially the nation Israel, (2) his ultimate seed, the Lord Jesus Christ, and (3) God’s blessing through Abraham upon all the families of the earth.

scriptures.text: “And I will make you a great nation, and I will bless you, and make your name great; and so you shall be a blessing; and I will bless those who bless you, and the one who curses you I will curse.  And in you all the families of the earth will be blessed”

. “Now lift up your eyes and look from the place where you are, northward and southward and eastward and westward; for all the land which you see, I will give it to you and to your descendants forever ”
. And He took him outside and said, “Now look toward the heav- ens, and count the stars, if you are able to count them. ”  And He said to Him, “So shall your descendants be”
. Now the promises were spoken to Abraham and to his seed.  He does not say, “And to seeds, ” as referring to many, but rather to one, “And to your seed, ” that is, Christ

catechisms.question: God justify the ungodly?
catechisms.answer: . 52 The only righteous basis upon which God may justify the ungodly is by imputing unto them Christ’s very own sinless righteousness. catechisms.question: What is sonship?
catechisms.answer: . 53 Sonship is our entirely safe and eternally secure inclusion among the children of the Heavenly Father.

scriptures.text: But as many as received Him, to them He gave the right to become children of God, even to those who believe in His name, who were born, not of blood nor of the will of the /f_l  esh nor of the will of man, but of God

. See how great a love the Father has bestowed on us, that we would be called the children of God; and such we are. For this reason the world does not know us, because it did not know Him.  Beloved, now we are children of God, and it has not appeared as yet what we will be. We know that when He appears, we will be like Him, because we will see Him just as He is

catechisms.question: What is sanctification?
catechisms.answer: . 54 Sanctiﬁ  cation is the renewal of our entire inner man after the image of God, so that we are enabled more and more to die unto sin and to live unto righteousness.

scriptures.text: Now may the God of peace Himself sanctify you entirely; and may your spirit and soul and body be preserved complete, without blame at the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ.  Faithful is He who calls you, and He also will bring it to pass

. For just as you presented your members as slaves to im- purity and to lawlessness, resulting in further lawlessness, so now present your members as slaves to righteousness, resulting in sancti/f_i  cation
. Pursue peace with all men, and the sancti/f_i  cation without which no one will see the Lord

catechisms.question: requires of mankind?
catechisms.answer: . 58 The duty which God requires of mankind is obedience to His revealed will.  It is man’s duty to obey God.  The reason for this is that God is the creator and man is a mere creature.  Because God created man, He therefore has “a right” to require what He will from man.  Because man is only a creature, he has “no right” to “go his own way and do his own will.”  No, the only “right” for man is to obey God (G. I. Williamson).

scriptures.text: He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does the LORD require of you but to do justice, to love kindness, and to walk humbly with your God
Behold, to obey is better than sacrifice, and to heed than the fat of rams.  For rebellion is as the sin of divination, and insubordination is as iniquity and idolatry”

catechisms.question: summarized?
catechisms.answer: . Exodus 20:1-17; Deuteronomy 5:6-21 60 God’s moral law is summarized in the ten commandments. catechisms.question: What does every sin deserve?
catechisms.answer: . 64 Every sin deserves God’s wrath and curse, both in this life, and that which is to come.  Although every sin deserves God’s wrath and curse, it may now be proper to remark . . . there never has been, since the fall of our first parents, an individual . . . of whom it  could be said, after he became capable of moral action, that he had committed but one sin. catechisms.attribution: (Adapted from C. H. Spurgeon’s, A Catechism, Q. 70)
catechisms.raw_text:
What is repentance unto life?
Well then, God has granted to the Gentiles also the 
repentance  that leads  to life” (NASB; Acts 11:18b).
Now when they heard this, they were pierced to the 
heart, and said to Peter and the rest of the apostles, 
“Brethren, what shall we do?” Peter said to them, “Repent, 
and each of you be baptized in the name of Jesus 
Christ for the forgiveness of your sins; and you will 
receive the gift of the Holy Spirit (NASB; Acts 2:37-38).
For the sorrow that is according to the will of God 
produces a repentance without regret, leading  to 
salvation, but the sorrow of the world produces death 
(NASB; II Cor. 7:10) . See verse 11 for the seven-fold 
character of the godly sorrow producing repentance.  
67 
Repentance unto life is the change of 
heart whereby out of a genuine grief 
and hatred of our sin, we turn unto 
God with the earnest intent to strive 
after new obedience. 
(Adapted from C. H. Spurgeon’s, A Catechism, Q. 70)

catechisms.question: redemption to us?
catechisms.answer: . 68 The ordinary outward means by which the Holy Spirit communicates the benefits of redemption to us are God’s Word (read, memorized, preached, and taught), Baptism, the Lord’s Table, prayer, and the fellow - ship of spiritually-minded believers.

catechisms.question: heard?
catechisms.answer: . 70 God’s Word is to be read and heard with preparation, prayer, and diligence. It is to be received with faith and love.  It is to be laid up in our hearts and practiced in our lives.

scriptures.text: Therefore, putting aside all malice and all deceit and hypocrisy and envy and all slander, like newborn babes, long for the pure milk of the word, so that by it you may grow in respect to salvation
. Open my eyes, that I may behold wonderful things from Your law
. Prov. 2:1-5 For indeed we have had good news preached to us, just as they also; but the word they heard did not profit them, because it was not united by faith in those who heard

catechisms.raw_text:
How is God’s Word to be read and 
heard?
Therefore, putting aside all malice and all deceit and 
hypocrisy and envy and all slander, like newborn babes, 
long for the pure milk of the word, so that by it you 
may grow in respect to salvation (NASB; I Pet. 2:1-2). 
Open my eyes, that I may behold wonderful things 
from Your law (NASB; Ps. 119:18). 
Prov. 2:1-5
For indeed we have had good news preached to us, 
just as they also; but the word they heard did not profit 
them, because it was not united by faith in those who 
heard (NASB; Heb. 4:2). 
70 
God’s Word is to be read and heard 
with preparation, prayer, and diligence. 
It is to be received with faith and 
love.  It is to be laid up in our hearts 
and practiced in our lives. 
(Adapted from Westminster Shorter Catechism, Q. 90)

catechisms.question: the Church universal?
catechisms.answer: . 71 According to God’s Word, the Church universal is the body and bride of the Lord Jesus Christ, the household of God, the pillar and support of the truth.

catechisms.question: local church?
catechisms.answer: . John 4:23-24; Acts 2:42; I Thess. 1:8; I Pet. 4:10-11 72 According to God’s Word, a local church is an assembly of mutually acknowledged Christians, organized in one locale under scriptural leadership, for the purposes of worship, apostolic instruction, prayer, fellowship, evange- lism, service, and the observance of the ordinances.

catechisms.question: a local church’s scriptural leaders?
catechisms.answer: . See I Peter 5:1-4 for the ministry of elders. See Acts 6:1-7 for the early church’s first ordination of Spirit-filled men who were not elders, but whose wise ministry it would be to assist the Apostles. 73 According to God’s Word, a local church’s scriptural leaders are elders, who oversee and shepherd (pastor), and deacons, who assist them. An able and faithful ministry, next to the sanctify - ing operations of the Spirit, is the greatest benefit that can be conferred upon a people  (Samuel Miller, in his inaugural address at the founding of Princeton Theological Seminary in 1812).

catechisms.question: church is to observe?
catechisms.answer: . I Corinthians 11:23-26 74 The ordinances a local church is to observe are baptism and the Lord’s table. An ordinance is a practice ordained by Jesus Christ in which His people are to use physical actions (immersion, eating, and drinking) and materials (water, bread, and the fruit of the vine) both to signify and to identify themselves with the spiritual realities of His redemptive work on their behalf .

catechisms.question: What is the ordinance of baptism?
catechisms.answer: . 75 The ordinance of baptism is the immersion in water of a new Christian, in the name of the Father, and the Son, and the Holy Spirit.

scriptures.text: Go therefore and make disciples of all the nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit
. But when they believed Philip preaching the good news about the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, they were baptized, men and women alike
”  And he ordered the chariot to stop; and they both went down into the water, Philip as well as the eunuch, and he baptized him
”  And he ordered them to be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ

catechisms.raw_text:
What is the ordinance of baptism?
Go therefore and make disciples of all the nations, 
baptizing them in the name of the Father and the Son 
and the Holy Spirit (NASB; Matthew 28:19).
But when they believed Philip preaching the good news 
about the kingdom of God and the name of Jesus Christ, 
they were baptized, men and women alike (NASB; Acts 8:12).
As they went along the road they came to some water; and 
the eunuch said, “Look!  Water!  What prevents me from 
being baptized?”  And he ordered the chariot to stop; and 
they both went down into the water, Philip as well as the 
eunuch, and he baptized him (NASB; Acts 8:36, 38). 
“Surely no one can refuse the water for these to be 
baptized who have received the Holy Spirit just as we 
did, can he?”  And he ordered them to be baptized in 
the name of Jesus Christ (NASB; Acts 10:47-48a).    
75 
The ordinance of baptism is the 
immersion in water of a new 
Christian, in the name of the 
Father, and the Son, and the Holy 
Spirit.

catechisms.question: What does baptism signify?
catechisms.answer: . 76 Baptism signifies our spiritual union with our Savior in His death, burial, and resurrection for the forgiveness of our sins.

scriptures.text: Or do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus have been baptized into His death? 4Therefore we have been buried with Him through baptism into death, so that as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, so we too might walk in newness of life. 5For if we have become united with Him in the likeness of His death, certainly we shall also be in the likeness of His resurrection
. Having been buried with Him in baptism, in which you were also raised up with Him through faith in the working of God, who raised Him from the dead
. Peter said to them, “Repent, and each of you be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins; and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit”

catechisms.raw_text:
What does baptism signify?
Or do you not know that all of us who have been baptized 
into Christ Jesus have been baptized into His death? 
4Therefore we have been buried with Him through baptism

newness of life. 5For if we have become united with Him in

likeness of His resurrection (NASB; Romans 6:3-5).
Having been buried with Him in baptism, in which you were 
also raised up with Him through faith in the working of God, 
who raised Him from the dead (NASB; Colossians 2:12).
Peter said to them, “Repent, and each of you be baptized in

catechisms.question: Table?
catechisms.answer: . 77 The ordinance of the Lord’s Table is our eating and drinking of bread and the fruit of the vine in fellowship together, in order to proclaim the Lord’s death, and to testify to our own individual partaking of all its saving benefits.

scriptures.text: While they were eating, Jesus took some bread, and after a blessing, He broke it and gave it to the disciples, and said, “Take, eat; this is My body. ” 27 And when He had taken a cup and given thanks, He gave it to them, saying, “Drink from it, all of you; 28 for this is My blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for forgiveness of sins
Is not the bread which we break a sharing in the body of Christ
For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord’s death until He comes

catechisms.question: Who may partake of the Lord’s Table?
catechisms.answer: . 78 Those who may partake of the Lord’s Table are those baptized believers, who have examined themselves to ensure that they have confessed to their Lord all known sin, and have purposed anew to live in all loving obedience to Him.

scriptures.text: Or do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into Christ Jesus have been baptized into His death? 4Therefore we have been buried with Him through baptism into death, so that as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, so we too might walk in newness of life

. Therefore whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner, shall be guilty of the body and the blood of the Lord. 28 But a man must examine himself, and in so doing he is to eat of the bread and drink of the cup. 29 For he who eats and drinks, eats and drinks judgment to himself if he does not judge the body rightly. 30 For this reason many among you are weak and sick, and a number sleep. 31 But if we judged ourselves rightly, we would not be judged

. If we confess our sins, He is faithful and righteous to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness. 10 If we say that we have not sinned, we make Him a liar and His word is not in us

Or do you not know that all of us who have been baptized into

an unworthy manner, shall be guilty of the body and the blood

obedience to Him.

catechisms.question: Lord comes?
catechisms.answer: . 83 When our Lord comes (1) those of us asleep in Jesus will return with Him to be rejoined forever to our res- urrected bodies, (2) those of us still living will be raptured with them to meet Him in the air, where (3) we all will be imperishably, immortally glorified when we see Him as He is.

catechisms.question: What will follow our glorification?
catechisms.answer: . 84 Following our glorification, we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ.

scriptures.text: Therefore we also have as our ambition, whether at home or absent, to be pleasing to Him. 10 For we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each one may be recompensed for his deeds in the body, according to what he has done, whether good or bad

. For no man can lay a foundation other than the one which is laid, which is Jesus Christ. 12 Now if any man builds on the foundation with gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, straw, 13 each man’s work will become evident; for the day will show it because it is to be revealed with fire, and the fire itself will test the quality of each man’s work. 14 If any man’s work which he has built on it remains, he will receive a reward. 15 If any man’s work is burned up, he will suffer loss; but he himself will be saved, yet so as through fire

catechisms.raw_text:
What will follow our glorification?
Therefore we also have as our ambition, whether at home 
or absent, to be pleasing to Him. 10 For we must all appear

recompensed for his deeds in the body, according to what

For no man can lay a foundation other than the one which 
is laid, which is Jesus Christ. 12 Now if any man builds on the 
foundation with gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, 
straw, 13 each man’s work will become evident; for the day 
will show it because it is to be revealed with fire, and the 
fire itself will test the quality of each man’s work. 14 If any 
man’s work which he has built on it remains, he will receive 
a reward. 15 If any man’s work is burned up, he will suffer

all appear before the judgment seat 
of Christ.

catechisms.question: behind after we are raptured?
catechisms.answer: . 85 Those left behind after we are raptured will enter into a period of seven literal years of terrifying, catastrophic ordeals of the wrath of God and of the Lamb.

scriptures.text: “Because you have kept the word of My perseverance, I also will keep you from the hour of testing, that hour which is about to come upon the whole world, to test those who dwell upon the earth”

. Then the kings of the earth and the great men and the commanders and the rich and the strong and every slave and free man hid themselves in the caves and among the rocks of the mountains; and they said to the moun - tains and to the rocks, “Fall on us and hide us from the presence of Him who sits on the throne, and from the wrath of the Lamb”

catechisms.raw_text:
What will happen to those left 
behind after we are raptured?
“Because you have kept the word of My perseverance, 
I also will keep you from the hour of testing, that hour 
which is about to come upon the whole world, to test 
those who dwell upon the earth” (NASB; Revelation 3:10). 
Then the kings of the earth and the great men and the 
commanders and the rich and the strong and every slave 
and free man hid themselves in the caves and among 
the rocks of the mountains; and they said to the moun -
tains and to the rocks, “Fall on us and hide us from the 
presence of Him who sits on the throne, and from the 
wrath of the Lamb” (NASB; Revelation 6:15-16).
85 
Those left behind after we are 
raptured will enter into a period 
of seven literal years of terrifying, 
catastrophic ordeals of the wrath of 
God and of the Lamb.

catechisms.question: wrath terminate?
catechisms.answer: . See Daniel 9:24-27 for how these seven years and their termination will apply to the nation Israel. See Revelation 19:11-21 for how these seven years will terminate for the Gentile nations of the world. 86 The seven years of God’s wrath will terminate with our Lord’s return to earth to rule and reign.

catechisms.question: reign upon the earth?
catechisms.answer: . 87 Our Lord will rule and reign upon the earth for a thousand literal years. “Thy kingdom come!”  This is the burden of our cries. Weary of man’s rule, we long for God’s.  Sick at heart with this world’s scenes of evil,—man spoiling man; man enslaving man; man wounding man; man defrauding man; man treading upon man;—we long for the setting up of the righteous throne.  Oh, what a world will this be, when man’s will as well as man’s rule shall be ex- changed for Christ’s rule and will; when God’s “will shall be done on earth even as it is done in heaven!” (Horatius Bonar, The Morning of Joy)

scriptures.text: Then I saw thrones, and they sat on them, and judgment was given to them. And I saw the souls of those who had been beheaded because of their testimony of Jesus and because of the word of God, and those who had not wor - shiped the beast or his image, and had not received the mark on their forehead and on their hand; and they came to life and reigned with Christ for a thousand years . 5 The rest of the dead did not come to life until the thou - sand years were completed. This is the first resurrection. 6 Blessed and holy is the one who has a part in the first resurrection; over these the second death has no power, but they will be priests of God and of Christ and will reign with Him for a thousand years

catechisms.raw_text:

reign upon the earth?

catechisms.question: earth?
catechisms.answer: . 88 Following our Lord’s thousand- year reign upon the earth, the Devil and his angels, and all whose names are not written in the book of life will be cast into the Lake of Fire forever.

scriptures.text: . . . the eternal fire which has been prepared for the devil and his angels
. And the devil who deceived them was thrown into the lake of fire and brimstone, where the beast and the false prophet are also; and they will be tormented day and night forever and ever
. And if anyone’s name was not found written in the book of life, he was thrown into the lake of fire

catechisms.question: the book of life?
catechisms.answer: . 89 The eternal future for those whose names are written in the book of life will be sinless, sorrowless, painless, tearless, deathless, inexpressibly glorious life in a new heaven and a new earth and a beautifully adorned, holy city, the new Jerusalem!

scriptures.text: 1 Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth; for the first heaven and the first earth passed away, and there is no longer any sea. 2 And I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, made ready as a bride adorned for her husband. 3 And I heard a loud voice from the throne, saying, “Behold, the tabernacle of God is among men, and He will dwell among them, and they shall be His people, and God Himself will be among them, 4 and He will wipe away every tear from their eyes; and there will no longer be any death; there will no longer be any mourning, or crying, or pain; the first things have passed away. ” 5 And He who sits on the throne said, “Behold, I am making all things new. ”  And He said, “Write, for these words are faithful and true. ”

catechisms.raw_text:
What will be the eternal future for 
those whose names are written in

1 Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth; for the first heaven

 2 And I saw the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out

catechisms.question: should we be doing?
catechisms.answer: . 90 Since we are looking for a new heaven and a new earth, we should be making every effort so that we will be found by our Lord to be without spot or blemish, and at peace.

scriptures.text: 13 But according to his promise we are waiting for new heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells. 14 Therefore, beloved, since you are waiting for these, be diligent to be found by him without spot or blemish, and at peace. 15 And count the patience of our Lord as salvation, just as our beloved brother Paul also wrote to you accord- ing to the wisdom given him

catechisms.raw_text:

catechisms.question: the eternal end?
catechisms.answer: . 91 The eternal end shall be that God shall be all in all, that the Lamb shall be served by us who see His face, and that these Divine Persons shall receive from every created thing blessing and honor, glory and power,  dominion and praise forever and ever.  Amen, and Amen.

scriptures.text: When all things are subjected to Him, then the Son Himself also will be subjected to the One who subjected all things to Him, so that God may be all in all
. There will no longer be any curse; and the throne of God and of the Lamb will be in it, and His bond-servants will serve Him; they will see His face, and His name will be on their foreheads

. And every created thing which is in heaven and on the earth and under the earth and on the sea, and all things in them, I heard saying, “To Him who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb, be blessing and honor and glory and dominion forever and ever. ” And the four living creatures kept saying, “Amen. ” And the elders fell down and worshipped

catechisms.raw_text:
Finally, Christians, what shall be 
the eternal end?
When all things are subjected to Him, then the Son Himself 
also will be subjected to the One who subjected all things to 
Him, so that God may be all in all (NASB; I Corinthians 15:26).
There will no longer be any curse; and the throne of God and 
of the Lamb will be in it, and His bond-servants will serve 
Him; they will see His face, and His name will be on their 
foreheads (NASB; Revelation 22:3-4).
And every created thing which is in heaven and on the earth 
and under the earth and on the sea, and all things in them, I 
heard saying, “To Him who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb, 
be blessing and honor and glory and dominion forever and 
ever. ” And the four living creatures kept saying, “Amen. ” And 
the elders fell down and worshipped (NASB; Revelation 5:13-14). 
91 
The eternal end shall be that God 
shall be all in all, that the Lamb 
shall be served by us who see His 
face, and that these Divine Persons 
shall receive from every created 
thing blessing and honor, glory 
and power,  dominion and praise  
forever and ever.  Amen, and 
Amen.
